narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and an upper-middle family income background, your law school application stood out in several ways, including your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the school. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The strongest push toward a failure prediction came from your Year 1 school decile ranking of 4th, which is below the average of about 5.7th place and signaled to the model early academic struggles relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 35, lower than the typical 36.7, added further weight toward expecting bar challenges, as it was the second-most influential factor pulling in that direction. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile of 5th—right around the average of 5.5—helped counterbalance this somewhat, nudging the prediction toward success and showing later improvement. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early performance and test score aligned more closely with those who face hurdles, even with your strengths elsewhere. While this is disappointing, it doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar profiles through targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your early-grade transcript if possible through additional coursework, and gathering strong letters highlighting your Year 3 progress and resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view significantly.
